#separator:: What is a deadlock and why is it harder to detect than a crash?
column2:
A deadlock occurs when two or more goroutines are waiting for each other to release resources, and none of them can proceed.

It is harder to detect than a crash because the application doesn't terminate with an error — instead, it silently stops processing requests (e.g. API timeouts).

Go's runtime only detects a deadlock when ALL goroutines are blocked. A partial deadlock (e.g. 2 out of 50 goroutines) remains undetected by the runtime.

#separator:: What are the four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur?
column2:
• Incorrect lock ordering: Two goroutines lock the same resources (A and B) in different order, leading to mutual blocking
  • Double locking: Locking the same Mutex twice from the same goroutine
  • Forgotten unlock: Forgetting to call Unlock(), permanently blocking access for other goroutines
  • RWMutex trap: A waiting writer blocks new readers, which with nested read calls can cause a deadlock

#separator:: What happens if two goroutines lock resources A and B in opposite order?
column2:
A mutual deadlock occurs. Goroutine 1 locks resource A and waits for B, while Goroutine 2 locks resource B and waits for A. Neither can proceed because each holds the resource the other needs.

Solution: always lock resources in the same order (e.g. A first, then B).

#separator:: How does a deadlock occur when the same goroutine calls Lock() twice on the same mutex?
column2: sync.Mutex in Go is not reentrant (does not support nested locking). When a goroutine calls Lock() on a mutex it already holds — it blocks forever, waiting for an Unlock() that will never happen because it is blocked itself.

#separator:: Why can forgetting Unlock() silently break an application?
column2:
If a goroutine doesn't call Unlock(), the mutex stays locked forever. Every subsequent goroutine trying to Lock() will block. The application doesn't crash — it just silently stops processing requests that use that resource.

Always use defer mu.Unlock() right after Lock().

#separator:: Why can RWMutex lead to deadlocks with nested read operations?
column2: When a goroutine holds an RLock() and tries to acquire another RLock(), while a writer is waiting for Lock() in between — a deadlock occurs. The writer blocks new readers, so the nested RLock() will never be granted, and the first RLock() will never be released.

#separator:: What happens when a writer is waiting in an RWMutex?
column2: When a writer is waiting for Lock() on an RWMutex, new readers (RLock()) are blocked — even if other readers are still active. This prevents writer starvation, but means a waiting writer halts all new read traffic.

#separator:: How can you detect a deadlock in Go (tools / symptoms)?
column2:
• Runtime: Go detects full deadlocks automatically: "fatal error: all goroutines are asleep - deadlock!"
  • Partial deadlock: Runtime won't detect it — symptoms are API timeouts, hanging goroutines
  • go vet / staticcheck: static code analysis
  • pprof: goroutine dump (/debug/pprof/goroutine) shows blocked goroutines
  • Race detector: go run -race detects races, though not deadlocks directly

#separator:: How would you design code to avoid deadlocks?
column2:
• Consistent lock ordering: always lock resources in the same order
  • defer Unlock(): release the lock immediately after Lock()
  • Minimize critical section: hold the lock for as short as possible
  • Avoid nested locks: don't call Lock/RLock while holding another lock
  • Prefer channels for complex pipeline coordination
  • Use context.WithTimeout for operations that might block

#separator:: What happens to sync.Mutex performance under high contention (e.g. 3400+ goroutines)?
column2:
sync.Mutex performance drops drastically when competing goroutines exceed ~3400. This is due to the overhead of runtime.gopark and expensive context switching by the scheduler.

Mutexes also scale poorly with a large number of CPU cores compared to lock-free solutions.

#separator:: When should you use sync/atomic instead of sync.Mutex?
column2:
For simple operations like counters, sync/atomic is 2-3x faster than Mutex.

Use sync/atomic for simple atomic operations on single variables (increment, load, store).
Use Mutex when protecting a larger critical section with multiple operations.

#separator:: Why can overusing Mutexes make Go code harder to maintain?
column2:
Mutexes require manual lock management, which is prone to human error — it's easy to forget Unlock() or misuse read/write locks in RWMutex.

Overusing Mutexes instead of channels for coordinating complex task pipelines makes the code less readable and harder for other engineers to understand.

#separator:: Why doesn't Go's sync.Mutex support Try-Lock or timeouts?
column2:
sync.Mutex is a low-level, intentionally minimalist primitive. It lacks:

Try-Lock: No native method to check if a lock is available without blocking the goroutine (can be implemented manually).

Timeouts: No way to specify a maximum wait time for a lock, forcing developers to use more complex patterns with context and select.

#separator:: What is priority inversion and how can it affect goroutines using Mutexes?
column2:
Priority inversion occurs when a low-priority goroutine holds a lock, preventing high-priority critical goroutines from executing.

Without proper priority inheritance protocols, Mutexes can make it impossible to guarantee task execution deadlines in time-constrained systems.
